title: Thirteen Awards Movies to Watch Out for, Oscar Do-Overs, and More From the Mailbag
format: Sean and Amanda review part one of HBO's Tiger Woods documentary, run down the National Society of Film Critics winners, map thirteen movies to catch up on before Oscar season, refight a stack of Best Picture mistakes, and open the mailbag. Plus: Tiger directors Matthew Hamachek and Matthew Heineman.

films.title: Tiger
films.note: Sean calls part one 90 minutes of highlight reel married to a father-son story, with Earl Woods playing Dr. Frankenstein to Tiger's monster. Amanda watched with her golf-obsessed husband narrating every shot. Both note it's light on scandal and heavy on golf, and they single out the final shot, a glimpse of the woman whose affair with Tiger becomes part two's story, as the film's best bit of filmmaking.

segments.heading: National Society of Film Critics Awards
segments.note: the season's first big critics' prize

segments.heading: The National Society's history of picking the 'cool kids'
segments.note: Sean and Amanda look back at past Best Picture winners

films.title: Parasite
films.note: Amanda's example of a National Society Best Picture pick that also went on to win the Oscar.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: Another case where the National Society and the Academy agreed.

films.title: Spotlight
films.note: Same case: both bodies picked the same winner.

films.title: The Rider
films.note: The group's 2018 Best Picture, directed by Chloé Zhao, who's now the frontrunner again with Nomadland.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: The 2017 National Society Best Picture; the Oscar that year went to The Shape of Water.

films.title: Goodbye to Language
films.note: Godard's 2014 pick, Amanda's example of how far left-of-center the group can go.

films.title: Inside Llewyn Davis
films.note: The 2013 National Society Best Picture.

films.title: Amour
films.note: The 2012 pick, in Amanda's rundown of the group's history.

segments.heading: Thirteen movies to catch up on before Oscar season
segments.note: Sean's playbook for the next two-plus months

films.title: One Night in Miami
films.note: Regina King's directorial debut: one long conversation among Muhammad Ali, Sam Cooke, Jim Brown, and Malcolm X on one night in 1964. On Amazon that Friday.

films.title: Promising Young Woman
films.note: Writer-director Emerald Fennell and Carey Mulligan were on the show a few weeks earlier; the movie moves to VOD this week after a limited theatrical run.

films.title: The Little Things
films.note: The new Denzel Washington movie, on HBO Max January 29th. Sean doesn't expect an Oscar push but is rooting for one.

films.title: Supernova
films.note: A small Bleecker Street drama with Colin Firth and Stanley Tucci as two lovers. Sean calls it a nice movie.

films.title: Malcolm & Marie
films.note: Zendaya and John David Washington in Sam Levinson's Netflix two-hander. Sean calls it this year's 1917: the late, noisy entrant nobody had seen until everybody suddenly had. It takes aim at movie journalists, which he expects to be divisive, and he thinks it might be a little overwritten.

films.title: Judas and the Black Messiah
films.note: HBO Max, February 12th. One of the two late arrivals Sean thinks reshapes the nominations, along with Malcolm & Marie.

films.title: Land
films.note: Robin Wright's directorial debut, in which she also stars. Theaters February 12th, also premiering at Sundance.

films.title: Minari
films.note: Come up on the show some 20 times already; finally reaches theaters February 12th.

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: The clear Best Picture frontrunner, per both hosts. Theaters February 19th.

films.title: The Mauritanian
films.note: Kevin Macdonald's film about a man imprisoned at Guantánamo Bay awaiting trial for years. Sean thinks the movie itself is just okay but singles out Tahar Rahim's performance as a likely nominee.

films.title: Cherry
films.note: The Russo brothers adapting Nico Walker's autofiction novel, with Tom Holland. Theaters February 26th, Apple TV+ March 12th.

films.title: The Father
films.note: Sean saw it at Sundance a year earlier and isn't as high on it as everyone else, though he agrees Anthony Hopkins is a lock for a Best Actor nomination. Sony Pictures Classics, notoriously hard to see.

films.title: The United States vs. Billie Holiday
films.note: Lee Daniels's new film starring Andra Day as Billie Holiday, recently picked up by Hulu from Paramount; expected February 26th.

segments.heading: Oscar Do-Overs
segments.note: mailbag: which Oscar win would you correct?

segments.heading: The Mailbag
segments.note: Sean and Amanda, with Bobby Wagner reading listener questions

films.title: Hillbilly Elegy
films.note: Amanda's aside during the Pacino discussion: dread at the inevitable campaign to finally give Glenn Close an Oscar for this one.

films.title: Full Metal Jacket
films.note: Sean's answer for best 50 percent of a movie: the Parris Island first hour, with R. Lee Ermey's drill sergeant grinding the recruits down, is the most-cited great first half in movies.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Amanda's best-half pick: everything up through the first performance of 'Shallow' is dynamite, Dave Chappelle's odd cameo included.

films.title: Sullivan's Travels
films.note: Sean's answer on directors who might get lost to streaming: Preston Sturges's foremost classic, hard to find and with no restoration push behind it.

films.title: The Lady Eve
films.note: Sean's runner-up Sturges pick, right behind Sullivan's Travels.

films.title: Casablanca
films.note: Bobby admits he's seen it once, in college, and it isn't important to him, which horrifies Sean and Amanda enough that they assign it as homework. It resurfaces later as Amanda's ultimate comfort movie.

films.title: The Palm Beach Story
films.note: Amanda's alternate Sturges homework suggestion for Bobby.

films.title: Hail the Conquering Hero
films.note: Another Sturges title Amanda floats for Bobby to watch.

films.title: Unfaithfully Yours
films.note: The third Sturges title on Amanda's list for Bobby.

films.title: Yojimbo
films.note: Named in passing as the film that introduced Mifune's Sanjuro character.

films.title: Sanjuro
films.note: Sean rewatched it recently; part of his answer, alongside Toshiro Mifune, to the favorite pre-1960 movie star question.

films.title: Bringing Up Baby
films.note: Amanda rewatched this Hepburn-Grant screwball comedy the week before; she's partial to the chemistry even if it's not her single favorite performance from either star.

films.title: His Girl Friday
films.note: Amanda's pure favorite of Cary Grant's performances.

films.title: The Awful Truth
films.note: Named alongside His Girl Friday and Bringing Up Baby as one of Grant's best.

films.title: Holiday
films.note: The listener's question was inspired by the Criterion Cary Grant collection; Amanda notes Holiday had been out of circulation until Criterion picked it up.

films.title: North by Northwest
films.note: One of Sean's favorite Cary Grant performances.

films.title: To Catch a Thief
films.note: Sean loves this one too, and later picks its French Riviera set as the movie set he'd most like to visit: hanging out with Cary Grant while Grace Kelly is being courted by Prince Rainier.

films.title: Gunga Din
films.note: Sean's evidence that Grant could have been an action star if he'd wanted to be.

films.title: Only Angels Have Wings
films.note: One of the wartime-adjacent films Grant made instead of continuing purely as an action star.

films.title: Penny Serenade
films.note: The film at the center of a Pretend It's a City anecdote: Fran Lebowitz calls it Penny Arcade, Scorsese corrects her, then claims it was Grant's only Oscar nomination. Sean fact-checks that last part as wrong.

films.title: None But the Lonely Heart
films.note: Grant's other Best Actor nomination, which Scorsese forgot: an obscure film few people have seen.

films.title: Dial M for Murder
films.note: Sean's wife Eileen's favorite movie, by his best guess, and one he'd mentioned back on the show's Hitchcock episode.

films.title: The Thin Man
films.note: Eileen's other longtime favorite, per Sean.

films.title: A Muppet Christmas Carol
films.note: Eileen's unhealthy addiction, rounding out her probable top three.

films.title: Pretty Woman
films.note: Amanda's guessed (and wrong, by Zach's account) answer for his favorite movie.

films.title: The Philadelphia Story
films.note: One of the classic films Amanda and Zach watched together early in their relationship.

films.title: Reds
films.note: Amanda and Zach's failed attempt to watch together. They never finished it.

films.title: Michael Clayton
films.note: Zach refused to rewatch this with Amanda during their George Clooney Hall of Fame episode and instead reenacted it, yelling 'I am Shiva, the god of death' repeatedly.

films.title: Trolls World Tour
films.note: Sean's guess for a genuine VOD hit this year, based on iTunes and Amazon charts.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: Another likely VOD chart-topper, per Sean.

films.title: The Croods: A New Age
films.note: Named alongside Trolls World Tour and Tenet as a probable VOD hit with no reliable tracking.

films.title: Wonder Woman 1984
films.note: Referenced via 'the whole Wonder Woman 1984 meltdown' over the studio not releasing box office or streaming numbers.

films.title: Ocean's Eleven
films.note: Amanda's pick for a movie set she'd want to visit: the Soderbergh version, for the fun of hanging out with 'cool people.'

films.title: Eyes Wide Shut
films.note: Sean's pick: a long, secretive shoot with two of his favorite stars in the midst of their own marriage, making a film about a marriage's possible dissolution.

films.title: On the Count of Three
films.note: Amanda's most-anticipated Sundance title: comedian Jerrod Carmichael's directorial debut.

films.title: Passing
films.note: Sean's most-anticipated: Rebecca Hall's directorial debut, starring Tessa Thompson, Ruth Negga, André Holland, Alexander Skarsgård, and Bill Camp, based on the Nella Larsen novella.

films.title: Prisoners of the Ghostland
films.note: Sean's pick: Sion Sono teaming with Nicolas Cage on a wild action movie.

films.title: In the Same Breath
films.note: A Nanfu Wang documentary about the pandemic, from the director of One Child Nation. Another Sundance breakout Sean says he couldn't recommend more, though it's devastating.

films.title: Summer of Soul (...Or, When the Revolution Could Not Be Televised)
films.note: Amanda's pick: Questlove's directorial debut, produced by her friend Joey Patel.

films.title: The Sparks Brothers
films.note: Amanda's other pick: Edgar Wright's long-awaited documentary about the band Sparks.

films.title: Singin' in the Rain
films.note: One of Sean's ultimate comfort movies, joyful in a way he puts on often.

films.title: Sense and Sensibility
films.note: Another Sean comfort pick, rewatched again that week.

films.title: True Romance
films.note: Sean's other kind of comfort movie: not soothing, exactly, but the one he turns to when he doesn't want to think.

films.title: Clerks
films.note: Sean watched this constantly from 1996 through 2004; a comfort rewatch.

films.title: The Big Lebowski
films.note: On Sean's comfort-movie list.

films.title: 12 Monkeys
films.note: Sean's 1 a.m. insomnia rewatch the night before recording.

films.title: All the President's Men
films.note: Also one of Sean's comfort movies, not soothing exactly, but comforting in the sense that 'they're gonna get the job done.'

films.title: Network
films.note: Also on Sean's comfort list, alongside True Romance, Clerks, and The Big Lebowski.

films.title: The Wife
films.note: Named in passing twice: once as another hard-to-see Sony Pictures Classics release, once in a bit of mailbag nostalgia alongside A Star Is Born.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: Named alongside The Wife and A Star Is Born in the same bit of mailbag nostalgia.

films.title: Sweet Smell of Success
films.note: Sean's example of how daring Burt Lancaster could be, willing to be hated on screen.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.guest: Matthew Hamachek and Matthew Heineman on

films.title: Tiger
films.note: The directors describe three months of trust-building to land subjects like Tiger's first girlfriend Dina Gravell and Navy course manager Joe Grohman, the two archival finds they consider most important (Earl Woods's 1996 Haskins Award speech and Gravell's home video of a teenage Tiger dancing), and why they wanted Tiger to have a 'voice' in the film even though he declined to participate.
